general_info: Event-B is a formal method for system-level modelling and analysis. Models are specified using contexts (defining sets, constants, axioms) and machines (defining variables, invariants, and events). Invariants express properties that must always hold, and events describe state transitions via guards (conditions for execution) and actions (state updates). Refinement allows stepwise development from abstract specifications to concrete implementations while preserving correctness. Proof Obligations (POs) are generated automatically to ensure invariants are preserved and refinements are valid.
pitfalls_rules:
1. When defining events, ensure that all actions preserve the invariants.
2. For each FUN, SAF, DEG, DEL requirement, there should be one or more invariants with the same name of the requirement appended by invariant name, e.g. FUN_1_inv_1, FUN_1_inv_2, FUN_2_inv_1, ...
3. For each EQP, ENV requirement, there should be one or more axioms with the same name of the requirement appended by axiom name, e.g. EQP_1_axm_1, EQP_1_axm_2, EQP_2_axm_1, ...
4. For each variable v in machine, include an invariant `v \in | \subset | \subseteq ...` to specify its type.
5. For each constant c in context, include an axiom `c \in | \subset | \subseteq ...` to specify its type.
6. Make sure sets are not misplaced into constants, e.g. sets are types that are capitalized.
7. If a constant `fun` is used, make sure define its type, use format fun(x) if x is an element, and fun[X] if X is a set.
8. Any variable, constant or parameter in event must have a corresponding invariant, axiom, or guard to define its type.
9. Do not include comments.
10. Try to use latex commands to represent special symbols, make sure that only commands with a corresponding symbol in Event-B are included, e.g. exclude commands like \bcmin, \quad, etc.
11. Clauses such as "if...else..." are not supported by Event-B, use two events with different guards to represent the logic.
12. (x,y) is not supported and you must use x |-> y.
